sections.title: Avoidance of property dispositions in insolvency does not apply to financial market transactions
sections.summary: This section explicitly states that certain insolvency avoidance rules (e.g., fraudulent preferences, priority of payments) do not apply to financial market transactions. These include market contracts, margin, default fund contributions, collateral arrangements, and qualifying property transfers. The section also specifies that profits from such transactions are recoverable from non-defaulters unless the court directs otherwise, but this does not apply to market contracts where the party is a financial market infrastructure acting under its rules.

sections.heading: Duties of a registered self-regulatory organisation
sections.title: Self-regulatory organisations must act in public interest and notify Commission of market risks
sections.summary: Self-regulatory organisations and trade associations must act in the public interest to ensure market integrity and investor protection. They must supervise members for fair, orderly markets and immediately notify the Commission of any matters adversely affecting market interests or investor rights. They must also notify the Commission of any member contraventions of the Act or regulations and provide affected members with representation opportunities before making decisions that impact their rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Self-regulatory organisations must promptly report market risks and contraventions to the Commission, requiring robust internal monitoring systems for cross-border financial data handling and market integrity.

requirements.text: Act in public interest to ensure market integrity and investor protection
Supervise members to ensure fair, orderly and efficient market
Immediately notify Commission of matters adversely affecting market or investor interests
Immediately notify Commission of member contraventions of Act or regulations
Provide affected member opportunity to make representations before decisions that adversely affect rights

sections.source_quote: 60. A self-regulatory organisation or trade association shall — (a) at all times act in the public interest to ensure the integrity of the market and the protection of investors; (b) supervise the conduct of its members to ensure a fair, orderly and efficient market; (c) immediately notify the Commission if it becomes aware of any matter which adversely affects or is likely to adversely affect the interests of the market or the investing public; (d) immediately notify the Commission if it becomes aware of any contravention by its members of any provision of this Act or the rules and regulations made under it; and (e) not make a decision under its rules that adversely affects the rights of a member unless it has given the affected member an opportunity to make representations to it about the matter.

sections.heading: Accounts to be kept by capital market operator
sections.title: Capital market operators must maintain detailed financial records
sections.summary: Capital market operators must keep accounting records that show transactions and financial position to enable true and fair profit and loss accounts and balance sheets. Records must be detailed enough to show all transactions, including money received/paid, securities transactions, income/expenses, assets/liabilities, securities property, and transactions with clients, the operator, and employees. Failure to comply results in fines of N2 million or imprisonment for one year.
sections.dtia_summary: Detailed financial record-keeping requirements ensure transparency in cross-border financial data handling and market operations.

requirements.text: Keep accounting records showing transactions and financial position to enable true and fair profit and loss accounts and balance sheets
Records must be detailed enough to show all transactions including money received/paid, securities transactions, income/expenses, assets/liabilities, securities property
Records must specify the day or period during which each transaction took place
Records must contain copies of acknowledgements of receipt of securities or documents of title to securities from clients

sections.source_quote: 63.—(1) A capital market operator shall keep or cause to be kept such accounting and other records — (a) as shall sufficiently show and explain the transactions and financial position of his business and enable true and fair profit and loss accounts and balance sheets to be prepared, regularly; and (b) in a manner that shall enable them to be conveniently and properly audited. (2) A capital market operator shall be deemed not to have complied with subsection (1) in relation to records unless the accounting and other records of the capital market operator — (a) are kept in sufficient detail to show particulars of all — (i) money received or paid by the capital market operator, including money paid to or disbursed from a trust account, (ii) purchases and sales of securities made by the capital market operator, the charges and credits arising from them, and the names of the buyers and sellers, respectively of each of those securities, (iii) income received from commissions, interest, and other sources, and all expenses, commissions, and interest paid by the capital market operator, (iv) the assets and liabilities, including contingent liabilities, of the capital market operator, (v) securities which are the property of the capital market operator showing by whom the securities or the documents of title to the securities are held and, where they are held by some other person, whether or not they are held as securities against loans or advances, (vi) securities that are not the property of the capital market operator and for which the dealer or any nominee controlled by the security dealer is accountable, showing by whom, and for whom, the securities or the documents of title to the securities are held and the extent to which they are either held for safe custody or deposited with a third party as securities for loans or advances made to the capital market operator, (vii) purchases and sales of options made by the capital market operator and all fees (being options money) arising from them, (viii) arbitrage transactions entered into by the capital market operator, and (ix) underwriting transactions entered into by the capital market operator; (b) are kept in sufficient detail to show particulars of every transaction by the capital market operator; (c) specify the day on which or the period during which each transaction took place; (d) contain copies of acknowledgements of receipt of securities or documents of title to securities from clients.

sections.title: Rules and regulations for capital markets

sections.source_quote: 355.—(1) The Commission shall make rules and regulations for the purpose of giving effect to the provisions of this Act and shall in particular and without prejudice to the provisions, make rules and regulations which may include the underlisted and other incidental matters — (a) to alter or modify the provisions of the Second Schedule to this Act; (b) prescribing the forms for returns and other information required under this Act; (c) prescribing the procedure for obtaining any information required under this Act; (d) requiring returns to be made within the period specified by any entity to which this Act applies; (e) prescribing the procedure and criteria for approval of mergers, takeovers, acquisitions and business combinations under this Act; (f) prescribing any fees payable under this Act; (g) prescribing the manner for regulating securitization transactions in Nigeria; (h) prescribing the procedure and criteria for regulating cross-border offerings, listing and trading of securities by foreign issuers, and free trade entities; (i) prescribing that the provisions of this Act shall not apply or shall apply with such modifications, if any, as may be specified in the rules and regulations to any person or class of persons or any securities or class of securities or to any transaction; (j) prescribing the information to be contained in any prospectus or offer documents filed under this Act; (k) prescribing the procedure, criteria for the authorisation, revocation and operation of collective investment schemes including prudential and product regulation; (l) prescribing the activities which constitute insider dealings the rules governing dealings in securities by insiders and defining the term 'insider dealings'; (m) concerning derivatives, derivatives markets or business, derivatives exchanges, derivatives market infrastructure, derivatives business operators, trade association of derivatives business operators, and preventing unfair derivatives trading practices; (n) concerning digital and virtual asset services and related activities; (o) prescribing the treatment of unclaimed money that remain unpaid after 24 months or other prescribed period, after their payment date; (p) prescribing the establishment of a national confiscation wallet, multi-party combination wallet; (q) prescribing the domestication of private keys; (r) without prejudice to the provisions of any law, specifying for the protection of investors, prescribing — (i) the returns that are required to be filed by all public companies, (ii) matters relating to the conduct of annual general meetings, extraordinary general meetings and other statutory meetings of public companies and collective investment schemes particularly relating to attendance, use of proxies, voting and other related matters, (iii) the form, manner and procedure for obtaining proxies including the information to be disclosed to investors before proxies are given by any person, (iv) corporate governance standards for public companies and regulated entities, (v) sustainability reporting guidelines and disclosures including social and environmental sustainability standards for public companies and regulated entities, (vi) the manner in which such matters shall be disclosed by the companies, and (vii) the matters to be disclosed relating to the issue of securities, transfer of securities and matters incidental to it; (s) prescribing the requirement for the identification of persons doing business with capital market operators; (t) prescribing as it deems fit, appropriate rules on treatment of dividends, unclaimed securities and certificates, and other matters incidental to it; (u) providing for anything requiring to be prescribed under this Act; and (v) for carrying out the principles and objectives of this Act. (2) The Commission shall in the exercise of powers to make rules in this section consult with stakeholders, and any regulation under this Act shall be deemed made 15 days after receipt by the Minister unless the Minister, before the expiration of the 15 days, directs that it be modified, amended or rescinded.
sections.relevance_reason: Rules for cross-border offerings require stakeholder consultation

sections.title: Penalties for obstructing Commission functions or record destruction
sections.summary: A person commits an offence if they wilfully obstruct the Commission's functions, destroy records with intent to mislead, provide false information, fail to comply with Commission directives, or engage in fraudulent activities. Penalties include fines of N10 million to N50 million and imprisonment of two to five years, with additional daily penalties for non-compliance with directives.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes penalties for actions that could undermine regulatory oversight, relevant to DTIA as it defines prohibited conduct affecting data integrity and compliance with regulatory directives.

requirements.text: Wilfully obstructing, interfering with or interrupting the Commission in performing functions commits an offence
Destroying records required by the Commission with intent to mislead commits an offence
Providing false, misleading or incomplete information commits an offence
Failing to comply with Commission directives incurs penalties of N5 million initially and N20,000 daily
Using devices or schemes to defraud commits an offence with penalties of N5 million and imprisonment of three years

sections.source_quote: 344. Except as otherwise specifically provided under this Act a person who — (a) wilfully obstructs, interferes with or interrupts the Commission in the performance of its functions or exercise of its powers under this Act commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine of not less than N10,000,000 or imprisonment for a term of not less than two years or both; (b) destroys any record which may be required by the Commission for the performance of any of its functions under this Act, with intent to mislead the Commission or to prevent or impede any investigation or inquiry under this Act, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine of not less than N20,000,000 or imprisonment for a term of not less than three years or both; (c) provides any information or furnishes any document which is false, misleading, or from which there is a material omission, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine of not less than N20,000,000 or imprisonment for a term of not less than three years or both; (d) fails to comply with any directive of the Commission made under this Act, is liable to a penalty of not less than N5,000,000 in the first instance and N20,000 for every day the violation continues; or (e) uses any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, or engages in any act, practice or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud on any person, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term of not less than three years or a fine of not less than N5,000,000 and in addition to such other penalty as may be prescribed under this Act and the rules and regulations made under it.

sections.title: Definitions of key terms in the Investment and Securities Act
sections.summary: This section provides comprehensive definitions for terms used throughout the Investment and Securities Act 2025. It establishes precise meanings for critical concepts such as 'agent', 'appropriate officer', 'approving authority', 'asset', 'associated person', 'auditor', and numerous other financial and regulatory terms essential for the operation of the capital markets and securities sector in Nigeria. These definitions clarify the scope and application of the Act's provisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines critical terms that may impact data transfers, particularly 'client trade', 'clearing member client contract', and 'collective investment scheme' which could affect how financial data is shared across borders in cross-border securities transactions.

requirements.text: agent means a person authorised by another to act for or in place of him
appropriate officer means the chief executive or any other officer authorised by the council of a state government, local government, or board of a statutory or government agency
approving authority in the case of the Federal Government means the Federal Executive Council
asset includes any real or personal property, whether tangible or intangible
associated person means a subsidiary, affiliate or agent of a member of any regulated entity

auditor means a member of a body of accountants recognised by an Act or any other enactment and appointed as auditor of a company or collective investment scheme by managers with the approval of the trustees
bid means an invitation or an offer
board of trustees refers to the board of trustees of an investor protection fund
bond means an instrument of indebtedness issued by a body to secure the repayment of money borrowed by such body
bond holder means a person holding a bond and whose name is entered as the owner in the register
book includes any register, document or other record of information and any account or accounting record

breach means a failure to comply with directives of the Commission, observe or give effect to the provisions of this Act or any rules made under this Act, in circumstances where there is an obligation to do so
capital market means a financial market that brings buyers and sellers together to trade securities, commodities, and other financial assets
capital market operator means any persons, individual or corporate, duly registered by the Commission to perform specific functions in the capital market
capital market participant means an investor, issuer, capital market operator, securities exchange, financial market infrastructure or any other person or entity participating in the capital market
cash means any cash equivalent other than physical cash
central clearing house means an entity responsible for settling trading accounts, clearing trades, regulating delivery, and reporting trading data
central counterparty means a central clearing house that interposes itself between counterparties to contracts traded in one or more financial markets
central securities depository means an entity that enables securities transactions to be processed and settled by book entry
certificate of registration means any certificate or license issued by the Commission as a part of its registration functions under this Act
cleared contract means a clearing member contract or a clearing member client contract
clearing house in relation to a commodity exchange means a body corporate that clears and settles commodity contracts
clearing member means a user of a central counter party or central clearing house authorised to perform clearing services
clearing member client contract means a contract between a financial market infrastructure and one or more clearing members' clients
clearing member contract means a contract between a financial market infrastructure and a clearing member
clearing and settlement company means any corporate body which acts as an intermediary in making payments or deliveries
client trade means a contract between two or more clearing members' clients or indirect clients
close-ended investment scheme means a pooled investment fund in whatever legal form which raises a fixed and irredeemable amount of capital
code means the Nigerian Takeover Code as may be formulated by the Commission
collateral management company means a company registered by the Commission to manage commodities as collateral
collateral security means any realisable asset provided under a charge or repurchase or similar agreement
collateral security charge means collateral security that consists of realisable assets, including money
collective investment scheme means a scheme or arrangement in whatever form, including an open-ended and close-ended investment scheme
commercial or investment business activities means any activity relating to micro, small and medium scale enterprise
Commission means the Securities and Exchange Commission
commissioner means the commissioner in a state responsible for matters relating to finance
commodity means — any produce, item, commodities, article, index, right, or interest of any nature
commodity contract means any contract in relation to a commodity
commodity exchange means — a market, whether in Nigeria or any other jurisdiction, registered or recognised by the Commission
commodity forward contract means a contract the effect of which is that one party agrees to deliver a specified commodity
company has the same meaning as defined in the Companies and Allied Matters Act, 2020
control means control of an entity by a person who — beneficially owns more than one half of the issued share capital or assets
corner means to acquire enough units of a security or to hold a significant commodity position to be able to manipulate its price
court means the Investments and Securities Tribunal, Federal High Court, or any other court of competent jurisdiction
credit enhancement service means any financial strategy or service provided by financial institutions or third parties to improve the credit rating
custodial agreement means the agreement drawn up between the custodian and other parties in a collective investment scheme
custodian means a person who has custody as a bailee of assets, securities or certificates issued in the name of the investor
dealer means a person engaged in the business of buying and selling of securities for own account
dealing member means a body corporate that is a member of a recognised exchange and is licensed to engage in dealing in securities on that exchange
dealing in securities means making or offering to make with any person, whether as principal or agent
default arrangement means a system put in place to limit systemic and other types of risk which arise in the event of a participant appearing to be unable
defaulter means a person in respect of whom action has been taken by a financial market infrastructure under its default arrangements
default proceeding means any action taken by a financial market infrastructure under its default rules
default rules mean the rules of a registered securities exchange or registered financial market infrastructure which provide for the taking of action in the event of a person appearing to be unable
delivery means voluntary transfer of possession or legal title from one person to another
depositor means any person who deposits a commodity in a warehouse for storage, handling, or shipment
depository means a custodian who holds securities on behalf of known investors but whose name appears on the issuer's register as a fiduciary nominee

derivatives means any financial instrument or contract that creates rights and obligations and whose market price, value, delivery or payment obligations are derived from, referenced to or based on the value of one or more underlying
director means a director as defined in the Companies and Allied Matters Act, 2 2020
eligible pledgee means any financial institution licensed by the Central Bank of Nigeria to among other things provide financing against securities
entity means any legal person incorporated under the Companies and Allied Matters Act, 2020 or registered under an Act of the National Assembly
exchange in relation to an investor protection fund means the securities exchange which established the fund
exchange holding company means a parent company of any entity approved as a securities exchange
executive council means the Federal Executive Council or the executive council of a state
executive officer in relation to a body corporate means any person by whatever name called and whether a director or not who participates in the management of the body corporate
expert means an engineer, legal practitioner, accountant or any other person whose profession gives authority to a statement made or action taken by him
facility when used with respect to an exchange includes its premises, tangible or intangible property
filing means delivery to the Commission through mails, electronically or otherwise of all papers or applications required to be filed with the Commission
financial market infrastructure means any entity set up to carry out centralised, multilateral clearing, settlement, caching or recording activities

financial sector regulator means any government authority, body, agency or entity within or outside Nigeria responsible for — monitoring, mitigating and managing systemic risk for promoting financial stability
general obligation debt securities means debt securities issued to finance the various projects of the government and repayable out of the general revenue and assets of the issuer
government securities means securities which are direct obligations of and whose principal and interest repayments are guaranteed by the Federal Government of Nigeria
holder in relation to collective investment scheme means any investor or beneficiary who has acquired units of a collective investment scheme
income accrual in relation to a collective investment scheme means any dividend or interest or profit or any other income for distribution received by the trustee

sections.title: Definitions of key financial market terms in Nigeria's Investment and Securities Act
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Investment and Securities Act, 2025. It specifies what constitutes an 'indirect participant', 'insider', 'insider dealing', 'insolvency proceedings', 'institution', 'internal control', 'investment adviser', 'investor protection fund', 'invitation', 'issuer', 'legal entity identifier', 'listed company', 'listing', 'listing rules', 'loan', 'manager', 'market charge', 'market collateral', 'market contract', 'market participant', 'market venue', 'member', 'merger', 'Minister', 'offer', 'offeree company', 'offeror', 'open-ended investment company', 'participatory interest', 'participant', 'penalty', 'person in breach', 'portfolio', 'portfolio investment', 'prohibited scheme', 'project-tied debt securities', 'promoter', 'promissory note', 'prospectus', 'public officer', 'qualifying property transfer', 'quotation', 'register', 'registrar', 'regulated entity', 'related company', 'rules', 'securities', 'securities dealer', 'securities exchange', 'securities holder', 'securities lending', 'securities settlements system', and 'securities transfer agent'. These definitions establish the legal framework for financial market operations in Nigeria.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are foundational for Data Transfer Impact Assessments as they clarify the scope of data subjects, data processors, and data flows within financial market infrastructure, particularly for cross-border transfers of securities-related data.

requirements.text: An 'indirect participant' is an institution, central counterparty, settlement agent, clearing house, or system operator with a contractual relationship to effect transfer orders through a system and whose identity is known to the system operator.

An 'insider' includes directors, employees, employers, persons in professional relationships, shareholders owning 5% or more of securities, audit committee members, and others with unpublished price sensitive information.
Insider dealing means buying or selling securities by an insider using confidential, non-public price sensitive information.
Insolvency proceedings cover judicial or administrative proceedings in Nigeria for bankruptcy or reorganization where assets are subject to tribunal control.

An 'institution' in relation to financial market infrastructure includes relevant capital market operators, public authorities, entities with foreign head offices matching those functions, and entities treated as institutions by the Commission.
Internal control refers to policies, procedures, and practices ensuring asset safety, financial record accuracy, corporate objectives, and compliance with laws and governance standards.

An 'investment adviser' is a person registered by the Commission to advise on securities, publish analysis, or make reports, excluding specific entities like Tranks, insurance companies, and newspaper proprietors.
An 'investor protection fund' is a fund established by an exchange to mitigate investor losses.
An 'invitation' is any statement offering securities to a person or class of persons.
An 'issuer' includes any person or entity issuing securities, managers of collective investment schemes, and bodies referred in section 268.
A 'legal entity identifier' is a code uniquely identifying every distinct entity in a financial transaction.
A 'listed company' is a company admitted to a securities exchange for trading and subject to regulatory oversight.
A 'listing' means admission of securities into an exchange's official list for trading and regulatory oversight.
Listing rules are regulations applicable to listed companies or exchanges, subject to Commission approval.
A 'loan' includes internal loans and credit facilities for making, accepting, repaying, or applying for loans.
A 'manager' is a fund or portfolio manager registered by the Commission.
A 'market charge' is a fee granted to financial market infrastructure for securing debts from market contracts.
Market collateral refers to one or more forms of security accepted by financial market infrastructure.
A 'market contract' includes contracts between market participants, exchanges, and financial market infrastructure for settlement and central counterparty services.
A 'market participant' is any person involved in capital market transactions under this Act.
A 'market venue' is any physical or virtual platform where securities and financial instruments are traded.
A 'member' is a company dealing in securities recognized as a member of a securities exchange.
A 'merger' means amalgamation, acquisition, or establishment resulting in joint ventures or control over businesses.
The 'Minister' refers to the Minister responsible for finance matters.
An 'offer' is any statement offering securities to a person or class of persons.
An 'offeree company' is a company whose shares are the subject of a take-over bid.
An 'offeror' is a person or group making a take-over bid.
An 'open-ended investment company' is a collective investment scheme with variable capital permitting share redemption.
A 'participatory interest' is any interest, unit, or share that may be acquired by an investor in a portfolio.
A 'participant' in relation to Part V includes institutions, system operators, bodies performing clearing functions, and indirect participants.
A 'penalty' means administrative fines imposed by the Commission.
A 'person in breach' is a person who contravenes directives or fails to comply with the Act or rules.
A 'portfolio' means a group of assets including cash.

sections.title: Definitions of key financial market terms
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Investment and Securities Act, 2025. It specifies what constitutes a settlement agent, share, significant market participant, stockbroker, surplus money, system, system operator, systemic risk, systemically important market participant, take-over, take-over bid, tradable instrument, trade repositories, trust account, trust deed, trustee, unclaimed money, underwriter, units, unit trust scheme, unpublished price sensitive information, warehouse, warehouse operator, warehouse operator's lien, warehouse receipt, and warehouse receipt system. These definitions establish the legal framework for financial market operations and regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: A settlement agent means a body corporate providing settlement accounts to institutions and registered financial market infrastructure for clearing and related functions
A share means a proprietary interest in the share capital of a body corporate
A significant market participant means a registered market operator meeting specific revenue, asset, or trading thresholds
A stockbroker means a member of a registered stockbrokers association and dealing member of a securities exchange
Surplus money means money payable to subscribers from over-subscription of an offer
A system means a formal arrangement for recording, clearing or settling financial transactions
A systemic risk means a situation where one or more events occur that threaten capital market stability
A systemically important market participant means a significant market participant designated by the Commission
A take-over means acquisition by one company of sufficient shares in another to give control
A take-over bid means an offer to acquire voting shares or voting rights in a company
A tradable instrument refers to securities, commodities, warehouse receipts, financing contracts, derivatives
Trade repositories means entities maintaining centralised electronic records of transaction data
A trust account means an account established under a trust deed or kept by a capital market operator
A trust deed means an agreement between trustee and manager of a collective investment scheme
A trustee means the corporate entity holding property subject to a trust created under a scheme
Unclaimed money means money due to investors from aborted offers or rejected applications
An underwriter means a person registered by the Commission who temporarily purchases securities
Units in a unit trust scheme means participatory interests in the scheme
A unit trust scheme means an arrangement providing facilities for public participation in a trust
Unpublished price sensitive information means information that relates to matters not of general nature
A warehouse means commercial space approved by the Commission for commodity storage
A warehouse operator means a person registered under this Act engaged in warehouse operations
A warehouse operator's lien means the right to recoup fees and charges for services rendered
A warehouse receipt means a document of title to commodities issued by a warehouse operator
A warehouse receipt system means a centralised electronic system for registration of commodity transfers

sections.summary: This section requires that when a business is proposed to be acquired, a report by accountants must detail the business's profits or losses over the five financial years preceding the statement and its assets and liabilities at the latest account date.

sections.title: Share acquisition financial report
sections.summary: This section requires that when shares in a body corporate are proposed to be acquired that will make it a subsidiary, a report must detail the profits and losses and assets and liabilities of the other body corporate, indicating how they would affect the acquiring company's members and what allowances would be made for other shareholders.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed financial reporting for share acquisitions, which impacts data transfers as it requires precise financial data to be shared across borders for regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: Where it is proposed to acquire share in a body corporate which by reason of the acquisition or anything to be done in consequence or in connection with it will become a subsidiary of the company, there shall be set out a report made by accountants (who shall be named in the statement) with respect to the profits and losses and assets and liabilities of the other body corporate in accordance with paragraph 3 (1) or (2) of this Schedule, as the case requires, indicating — (a) how the profits or losses of the other body corporate dealt with by the report would in respect of the shares to be acquired, have concerned members of the company; and (b) what allowance would have fallen, to be made in relation to assets and liabilities so dealt with, for holders of other shares, if the company had at all material times held the shares to be acquired

sections.heading: Any report by accountants required by Part II of this Schedule shall be
sections.title: Accountant qualifications for reports
sections.summary: This section specifies that reports required by Part II of the Schedule must be made by accountants qualified under the Act for appointment as auditors of a company and prohibits accountants who are officers, servants, partners, or employees of the company or its subsidiaries from making such reports, with 'officer' including proposed directors but excluding auditors.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that financial reports are prepared by independent auditors, which is critical for data transfers involving financial information to maintain integrity and avoid conflicts of interest.

requirements.text: Any report by accountants required by Part II of this Schedule shall be made by accountants qualified under this Act for appointment as auditors of a company

Any report by accountants required by Part II of this Schedule shall not be made by any accountant who is an officer or servant, or a partner of or in the employment of an officer or servant of the company, or of the company's subsidiary or holding company or of a subsidiary of the company's holding company
For the purposes of this paragraph the expression "officer" shall include a proposed director but not an auditor

sections.source_quote: 7. Any report by accountants required by Part II of this Schedule shall be made by accountants qualified under this Act for appointment as auditors of a company and shall not be made by any accountant who is an officer or servant, or a partner of or in the employment of an officer or servant of the company, or of the company's subsidiary or holding company or of a subsidiary of the company's holding company; and for the purposes of this paragraph the expression "officer" shall include a proposed director but not an auditor.
